speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two with Jacques Rosenthal. OK, Jacques, please continue. You were telling me about this-- this job that you took.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a different porter what you have. The concept of a porter is different in France than here. My job consisted, as a porter, to, when you check in your luggage, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and you have to bring it to the train that leaves in the next hour or two hours later. So I didn't have anything to do with public. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So my job, I wasn't 17-- I was not 17 years old. They used to pay us every day. And the day that I received my pay, I could eat. But one day I could eat, and the next day I couldn't pay my rent. [LAUGHS] So-- I was just going to ask, where were you living now? You left your family, your relatives.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I left the family, and I-- I was living in [INAUDIBLE] with all the students. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and I was-- 8:00, I had to be in school. So by the first metro, I was home the next 10 minutes, I had time to wash up and look over [LAUGHS] what I have to do for school and run to school. My school was away from the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had-- my best time was when I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I grew up. I don't say mature, but I grew up when I was a young man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I made it. I was making 17 francs a day. And when I graduate, on the same station, I had that job. I was paid by the month, 1500 francs a month.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was one of the-- I was privileged in my life to be [LAUGHS] number one. I was number one when they installed, first, electrical signals for this-- for the trains. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I spent on the same station, where-- where I used to make 17 dollars every day-- 17 francs, not dollars. I-- I became a big shot with 1500 francs a month. After three or four months, I received another assignment, to build between-- in the mid-France, between the town of Nivea and Vichy, the first electrical signals. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I moved to Vichy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in Vichy, they hired lots of people that they couldn't get any permit to live in Paris. And mostly they were students. They establish a residence in Vichy, and we would hire them. So we hired about 150 to 200 people, all in it. And there were four people divided on a 60 kilometer's distance to install the basic [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] signals, what's called crocodile. Crocodile is, in the train, when the train goes over the crocodile, it brushes and it makes the contact-- electric contact. And the signal function this way. So but the train has to go through, and in the end the train, there's-- every wagon had a brush that hit the crocodile. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So this was my-- this was already until 1931. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So the school, I graduate, and I was also privileged to go with one of the biggest mathematicians in the school, with his son, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In the same school. There was Conservatoire [? Nacional, ?] and the Arts et Metiers. It's a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's a very popular engineering school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Otherwise in that time, they didn't have any engineering school by state, by government. The universities belonged to the state. Were all private schools, beside Ecole Polytechnique, that you could get the engineering diploma. But that was for high ranking officers only. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to school with this young boy and became part of their family. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And their family, the father-- Paul [INAUDIBLE] was his name. He was Minister of War. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had suspicion that the man is Jewish. But officially, we didn't know. I didn't know till after World War II.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the school where he was teaching, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they made the pavilion, and the pavilion exists still today. And in the pavilion, it's called-- the building, the whole building-- the faculty of [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: I have a question. During this time that you left your family and went to France, up until now, this time that you're in this engineering school, did you ever practice Judaism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't ashamed. I wasn't afraid. I was actively involved in a student organization. I was actively interested. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I like to listen to a good cantor. Anytime I knew or heard there's a cantor, a new cantor, I used to go there for my own. I don't-- I'm not buying, I'm not selling in my religion. It's for me. And I felt very close to-- and I was involved in-- told you I was involved in politics for Israel, for Palestine in that time. So mainly-- and also, I joined in that time the Human League, the Ligue des Droits de l'Homme. And this was-- for me, it was, any time they hit a Jew someplace, this League [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was always protesting. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember there was a case that, in Brazil, at University of Brazil, a Jew was killed-- a student. And we went to protest with the League to the Brazilian consulate and to-- [INAUDIBLE] arrested us. But I was always interested in my cause. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And this-- I cannot separate myself from my religion. Not so.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'd mentioned to me that you joined up with the military, with the French military? You joined up. You wanted to become more actively involved with Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but that was part of my Boy Scout [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell me tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a Boy Scout like anybody else. But between the Boy Scouts, the Jewish Boy Scouts, there was different segment of Boy Scouts. There was segment of Boy Scouts that they were all pro communist. And the segment was-- on the other side of pro communist was [LAUGHS] pro Palestine. And the same thing like the political groups what we had in-- in that time. We had the Labor Movement. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You have groups in the Labor Movement, and one went-- went extreme. Were pro communist. Or the still called [INAUDIBLE], but it was a different segment. And there was a segment that I joined, mostly, they were pro Palestine. And that was the beginning of the Likud movement. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The head man of the Likud movement. Was a fellow by the name [INAUDIBLE] Jabotinsky. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as having knowledge of Hebrew in France, every time he came to Paris, I was his secretary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's why I was always involved [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the movement, and I never believed they will give us something for nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So this is the movement that I joined, and this is the movement that I, till today, believe in it. And how old were you when you joined this movement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe 15 or 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this started earlier on, just soon after you came to Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. After I came to Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this job you had. You were working at the power station.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was already in 19-- from '40 till '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was much later. What-- let's go back then. What happened between the time of '31 and '41?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I graduate. I was sent to Germany-- back to Germany, to Siemens, for experience. In France in that time, you couldn't a diploma. You had to have practical work for one year. And knowing German, and it was a brand new Siemens, was a brand new in France. There was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a frozen situation between 1919 till 1930 between France and Germany. But finally they broke the ice, and Siemens established the first. And there I was hired by Siemens without knowing that they want me to go to Germany. But I spent in Paris for two or three months with them, and then they say if I'd like to go. And they sent-- and so I went to Tempelhof. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I spent there-- and I fell into the medical division. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And in that time, we worked on sound equipment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And today, you have sound equipment that there's 10 million vibrations in a second. And in that time, the vibration was only 200-- between 200 and 300 vibration in a second. And that's how I fell into sound. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a gift in me without going to school for vision in touch. I used to-- I hear-- until today I hear music by touching [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] vibrations. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So-- then between that time, I find a job. I came back to Paris. I had already diploma and I find a job with the company that was called, in that time, the Materiel Telephonique. They owned the telephone company. But that was really owned by Western Electric. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I was with them maybe two or three months. And I was transferred from Paris to London-- the headquarters was in London-- to Western Electrical, the same company that owned the telephone company in France. Since that time, already in 1952 or '52-- '31 or '33-- they changed the law that you cannot have a foreign company. You must have 51% of the company must be French. So that's how this switched me over to the office in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you. We talked in our pre interview a little bit about your activities involved with the US colony as a courier, and your involvement with the shah and all those things.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. That's later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: This happened in the mid '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But prior to this-- It's not by choice. [? Whatever ?] happened to me, it's not by choice. I just fell in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] June the 11, 1926, I had lunch in a restaurant in Paris. And all of a sudden, we heard gunshots. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And later on, we didn't know what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all the people that are going in-- in this restaurant were taken to the police station. And in the police station, we heard of a name-- a fellow by the name Schwarzbad [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] killed a General from Russia that was-- his name was General Petliura.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- this Schwarzbad was from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from-- from the border of Russia and Poland. And his mother was killed by the soldiers from his army, Petliura. And he hunted the guy for many years until he finds. And he killed the guy. And I was supposed to be a witness. I didn't see the killing. I heard only. But it was a big trial in France. And by laws-- the French laws-- this Schwarzbad was exonerated. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And Schwarzbad died a few years back here in the United States. He was a watchmaker, and he was living in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I want to-- I want to stick, now, to your story. So this happened in 1936? This Schwarz--

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- no, this-- this happened in 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: '26. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to know what happened now in 19-- in the 1930s.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We're having knowledge of-- in the 1930s, late 1930s, having knowledge of English. I learned already English [INAUDIBLE]. I became actively involved with the-- the American colony in Paris. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And I'll show you pictures of a friend of mine that was a director of the United States line. He was also born in Warsaw. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Very fine fellow. He's still alive, I think, today in Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: And through him, I became friendly with the whole office in the American Legion. And somebody proposes to me. Said, would you like to go on a trip? I heard a trip, I said yes without knowing. So two or three months later, they're giving me an assignment [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to bring somebody to Yugoslavia in that time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I accomplished the assignment. They paid my pare, paid whatever expenses that I had, and that's all. And that's how I reached a day to go to Persia in that time. It was prior to the War, World War II. And I went there to Persia to work on security. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was also from Western Electric. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: In the palace of the Shah of [INAUDIBLE], and in September 1938, I think. It was his birthday-- 50th anniversary of Shah [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And while I was in Persia, I looked around. I went to any place. I remembered I was supposedly born in [? Purim. ?] This story of Queen Esther. And there was a-- there's a city in Persia by the name Hamadan. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is a big petroleum center. So I went to see the Hamadan, and I went to the cemetery. And I looked and I looked and I looked, and I find-- [LAUGHS] I find a grave by the name Haman. That's how I associate Hamadan. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I became allergic. You know, the water in that time used to come from the mountains when the snow melt-- melted. And anything that I touched in that time smelled with lamb and mutton. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I had a contract for two years. So that was the first six months. And I couldn't eat. Whatever I-- whatever I touched, even caviar-- [INAUDIBLE] the bread smells for lamb or mutton. So they let me out. They said, go out and-- so the doctor advised me to go to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in [INAUDIBLE], in-- from Damascus. It's high in the mountains. They said, go spend two or three weeks, whatever you want, and come back. Maybe you'll make a change. So I spent there three weeks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I came back to-- to Iran, and the same thing. I couldn't-- so they let me out in November, in '38. I went back to Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me also in our pre interview about-- you were in Perpignan, in 1937, was it, with the Lincoln Brigade?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in '38 as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Also I had an assignment from-- from the French government to-- when the war broke out with Spain, with Franco, a year and a half later, England and France declared a blockade against Spain. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was-- and all the Spaniards, that they came back after the fall of Madrid, they were living in a camp in Perpignan. And the camp, the name of the little town was Rivesaltes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I was in charge to bring out the people that were in camp-- the Lincoln Brigade, what was leftover. Original, the Brigade had 4,500 people. And to the-- to bringing them out, there were only 1,400 left. Lots of victims. Mostly [INAUDIBLE] in Spain. I was in charge to-- where the special train. And in three day-- three nights and four days we came to [INAUDIBLE], and aboard ship, they went back to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you mentioned that there was a famous photographer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know about it. But in the '50s or in the '60s, they had a show, [? Capa. ?] And I saw-- I saw the train. [LAUGHS] The show was not here. It was in Santa Barbara. We went to Santa Barbara to see the show. So then I realized that he was a filmmaker. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is out from Perpignan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he photographed the train that you took the Lincoln Brigade on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The train. We saw the people going into the train, different-- different four or five shots from the train. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So not by choice. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now tell me about what-- what else was going on at that time. You helped out with the Lincoln Brigade. What other kinds of things were you doing around that time-- 1938, 1939, just prior to the War breaking out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who? I came back. I came back. I came back in September-- in September or beginning of October in '38 from Persia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that was-- I was freelancing for nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the-- in 1938, after Munich-- after Munich, most of the people saw the war coming. And you know, there's Chamberlain, shalom, or peace. I suppose they have sacrificed Czechoslovakia for it. But everybody saw it coming. A war with Hitler. We didn't think about Russia in that time. But Hitler was already in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think at that time? What were you thinking at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very hard. I-- it's the same thing what I think now about Israel. I cannot separate my wishful thinking to reality. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So everybody suddenly have, again, shalom in that time with Chamberlain and [INAUDIBLE]. I didn't believe in it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I had hopes maybe. The same thing, I had hopes for 49 years. I had hoped for shalom. I know shalom cannot exist, though.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to go on to the next tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Can I [INAUDIBLE]?
